name: Tartaria Full
description: The 'Tartaria Full' theory posits the existence of a vast, globally dominant empire called Tartaria (or Great Tartary) that was deliberately erased from mainstream history. Proponents believe Tartaria was a technologically advanced civilization with a unique architectural style, free energy systems, and a sophisticated understanding of mathematics and science. They argue that Tartaria was overthrown by a global conspiracy, often attributed to the New World Order, Freemasons, or other shadowy groups, who then rewrote history to conceal its existence. Evidence cited includes inconsistencies in historical maps (particularly those showing a large 'Tartary' region in Central Asia), architectural anomalies in buildings around the world (often featuring 'star forts' and ornate designs), and alleged discrepancies in historical accounts. Mainstream historians dismiss the Tartaria theory as a pseudoscientific conspiracy theory lacking credible evidence. They point out that 'Tartary' was a term used by Europeans to describe a vast, poorly understood region of Central Asia inhabited by various nomadic groups, not a single, unified empire. The architectural features attributed to Tartarian technology are explained by conventional architectural history and engineering. The supposed historical inconsistencies are viewed as misinterpretations or fabrications. The theory's popularity is often linked to distrust of authority and a desire for alternative explanations of history.
subtitle: Lost Empire Conspiracy Theory

claims.claim: A global empire called Tartaria existed and was deliberately erased from history.
claims.evidence: Old maps labeling a 'Tartary' region, architectural similarities in buildings across the globe, alleged historical inconsistencies.
claims.criticism: Maps labeled areas with limited European knowledge. Architectural similarities are explained by common architectural styles and engineering. Historical inconsistencies are misinterpretations.

claims.claim: Tartaria possessed advanced technology, including free energy systems.
claims.evidence: Speculative interpretations of architectural designs, particularly 'star forts' and domes, as evidence of advanced energy systems.
claims.criticism: These architectural features are explained by conventional engineering and defensive strategies. No credible evidence of advanced energy systems exists.

claims.claim: A global conspiracy orchestrated the downfall of Tartaria and rewrote history.
claims.evidence: Alleged manipulation of historical records, destruction of Tartarian artifacts, and suppression of information.
claims.criticism: Lack of verifiable evidence. Attributing historical events to a single, overarching conspiracy is an oversimplification of complex historical processes.

keyFigures.name: Anatoly Fomenko
keyFigures.role: Promoter of 'New Chronology', which is linked to Tartaria theories

keyFigures.name: Philipp Melanchthon
keyFigures.role: Early geographer, whose maps are misinterpreted by proponents.

keyFigures.name: Jason Colavito
keyFigures.role: Researcher and critic of pseudohistory and conspiracy theories.

mythologyConnections: Connections to myths of lost golden ages and advanced civilizations.
Resonance with narratives of suppressed knowledge and hidden truths.

timeline.event: European cartographers label a large region of Central Asia as 'Tartary'.
Anatoly Fomenko develops 'New Chronology', questioning conventional historical timelines.
The 'Tartaria' theory gains popularity online, fueled by social media and alternative history communities.

sources.author: Jason Colavito
sources.work: Knowing Fear: Science, Knowledge, and the Development of the American Occult
sources.title: Jason Colavito

sources.author: Brent Allan Winters
sources.work: New Templars: America's Masters of Money
sources.title: Brent Allan Winters
